text:
Today being the thirteenth of June, you have caught me right in the thick of the season for the holiday I'd most like to invent: National Almost Day, observed every year on the eve of some other, better-known occasion, which is to say it floats around the calendar and nobody is ever quite sure when it falls until it has already passed. That ambiguity is the whole point. National Almost Day honors all the things we very nearly did, the plans we got eighty percent of the way through, and the heartfelt intentions that died gloriously on the launchpad.

Celebration begins, traditionally, by oversleeping. You set an alarm with great ceremony the night before, and then you do not get up when it rings, because rising promptly would dishonor the spirit of the day. Once you eventually stir, you greet your household with the official salutation, which is a thumbs-up followed by a small apologetic shrug. No words are required, and frankly words would be trying too hard.

The centerpiece of the festivities is the Almost Feast. Everyone is expected to begin preparing an ambitious dish, get distracted somewhere around the second step, and serve whatever resulted. A roast chicken becomes a sandwich. A three-tier cake becomes a bowl of batter eaten thoughtfully with a spoon. Guests compliment not the food but the concept of the food, raising their glasses to toast what the meal could have been under slightly different circumstances. The traditional beverage is a cup of coffee you made and then forgot about, drunk lukewarm, with dignity.

Gift-giving follows. The custom is to give someone a present you genuinely meant to give them on a previous occasion, still in its original bag, with the receipt regrettably still inside. There is no wrapping, because you ran out of time, which is celebrated rather than excused. The recipient must say "oh, you shouldn't have," and on National Almost Day this is understood to be literally accurate.

The day winds down with the reading of the Resolutions That Were, in which each person solemnly recounts a goal they set earlier in the year and describes, in loving detail, exactly how close they came before life intervened. The marathon that became a brisk walk. The novel that became a strongly worded group text. The community gently murmurs "so close" after each one, and it is the warmest sound you will hear all year. Then everyone goes to bed early, intending to do more tomorrow, which of course they almost certainly will.
